prompt: Explain what a "closure" is in programming, in exactly one sentence. The sentence can be long.
response: A closure in programming is a function that has access to its own scope and the outer scope of a function, even after the outer function has returned, thereby giving the closure access to variables that would otherwise be out of scope. This makes closures useful for implementing data encapsulation, stateful computations, and other functional programming patterns. The typical structure of a closure is as follows: define a function inside another function, and when calling the outer function, call the inner one and return its reference, so you can invoke it later to continue processing. This can reduce code complexity and improve code readability by grouping related items together. Closures use state information from one function for another function, and can also limit the scope of some variables in a declared function. It effectively captures a closure of a function, allowing its data to remain even after the function returns. Despite this power, closures create new scopes that are subject to rules about variable references. In other words, a closure enables you to store the intermediate result of a function, so when you call an outer function that enters another level scope and the execution completes, the closure can remember the status at the previous point. That is, a function returning a closure captures the outer function’s environment when it is defined, and it's that local environment later. This means that any variables kept alive by such a closure effectively become global, because the closure can access it at any time later on. If a function bound to one closure is called again, it will run in another environment. The big question is how can we encapsulate code that is available inside a closure, so it can work on a state variable without synthesizing it?
